questions.question: Match each patient case with their key memory deficit.
categories: Anterograde amnesia with spared nondeclarative memory
Anterograde + retrograde episodic amnesia with intact semantic
Impaired semantic knowledge with normal episodic
Impaired episodic with normal semantic development

Anterograde amnesia with spared nondeclarative memory
Anterograde + retrograde episodic amnesia with intact semantic
Impaired semantic knowledge with normal episodic
correct_mapping.Beth/Jon/Kate: Impaired episodic with normal semantic development

questions.explanation: H.M. had bilateral MTL removal causing anterograde amnesia but spared nondeclarative memory. K.C. had hippocampal damage causing both anterograde and retrograde episodic amnesia with intact semantic. A.M. had semantic dementia with preserved episodic memory. Beth/Jon/Kate had developmental hippocampal damage impairing episodic but not semantic memory.

questions.question: Match each MTL subregion with its primary characteristic.
categories: Slow, relational, spatial, recollection
Fast, item-based, novelty, familiarity
questions.explanation: The hippocampus is characterised by slow processing, relational binding, spatial processing, and supporting recollection. The perirhinal cortex is characterised by fast processing, item-based representations, novelty detection, and supporting familiarity.

questions.question: Match each type of consolidation with its characteristics.
categories: Fast (~90 min), occurs at the synapse
Slow (~28 days), MTL to cortex transfer

correct_mapping.Synaptic consolidation: Fast (~90 min), occurs at the synapse
correct_mapping.System consolidation: Slow (~28 days), MTL to cortex transfer

questions.explanation: Synaptic consolidation is rapid (about 90 minutes) and strengthens connections at the synapse level. System consolidation is slow (about 28 days) and involves the gradual transfer of memory representations from the medial temporal lobe to distributed cortical networks.

questions.question: Match each memory type with its definition.
categories: Events at specific place and time
Facts and general knowledge
Full contextual details of a memory
Sense of knowing without specific details

correct_mapping.Episodic memory: Events at specific place and time
correct_mapping.Recollection: Full contextual details of a memory
correct_mapping.Familiarity: Sense of knowing without specific details

questions.explanation: Episodic memory stores events tied to specific contexts. Semantic memory stores general facts and knowledge. Recollection involves rich contextual retrieval. Familiarity is a sense of having encountered something without remembering details.

questions.question: Match each PPC region (AToM model) with its associated function.
categories: Familiarity, low-confidence, top-down attention
Recollection, high-confidence, bottom-up attention
questions.explanation: In the AToM (Attention to Memory) model, dorsal PPC is associated with familiarity-based recognition, low-confidence judgments, and top-down attentional search. Ventral PPC is associated with recollection, high-confidence recognition, and bottom-up attention captured by retrieved memories.

questions.question: Match each type of priming with its neural basis.
categories: Sensory cortices
Prefrontal and lateral temporal cortices
questions.explanation: Perceptual priming depends on sensory cortices and is spared in amnesia. Conceptual priming depends on prefrontal and lateral temporal cortices, is spared in amnesia but impaired in Alzheimer's disease.

questions.question: Match each consolidation theory with its key claim about remote episodic memories.
categories: Remote memories become hippocampus-independent
Episodic memories always require the hippocampus

correct_mapping.Multiple Trace Theory: Episodic memories always require the hippocampus

questions.explanation: Standard Consolidation Theory proposes that over time, memories are transferred from hippocampal to cortical storage and become hippocampus-independent. Multiple Trace Theory (Nadel & Moscovitch) argues that episodic memories always depend on the hippocampus, with older memories simply having more distributed traces.

questions.question: Match each hippocampal theory with its main claim.
categories: Hippocampus forms new associations between items
Hippocampus supports episodic but not semantic memory

correct_mapping.Relational Memory theory: Hippocampus forms new associations between items
correct_mapping.Episodic Memory theory: Hippocampus supports episodic but not semantic memory

questions.explanation: The Relational Memory theory proposes the hippocampus is crucial for binding together new associations between items or elements. The Episodic Memory theory proposes the hippocampus specifically supports episodic memory (events) but is not required for semantic memory (facts).

questions.question: Match each three-phase model stage with its description.
categories: Formation of cortical traces + hippocampal indices
Consolidation of memory traces
Cue activates HC index which reactivates cortical traces

correct_mapping.Encoding: Formation of cortical traces + hippocampal indices
correct_mapping.Retrieval: Cue activates HC index which reactivates cortical traces

questions.explanation: In the three-phase model: encoding creates cortical traces with corresponding hippocampal indices; storage involves consolidation that strengthens these traces; retrieval uses a cue to activate the hippocampal index which then reactivates the distributed cortical representations.

questions.question: Match each memory taxonomy category with its examples.
categories: Episodic + Semantic
Priming + Skill learning + Conditioning
questions.explanation: Declarative (explicit) memory encompasses episodic memory (events) and semantic memory (facts), requiring conscious recollection. Nondeclarative (implicit) memory includes priming, skill learning (procedural), and conditioning, which operate without conscious awareness.
